lesson_title: Introduction to loops and while statements

cards.front: What is a loop in programming?
cards.back: A control flow construct that repeatedly executes a sequence of statements until some condition is no longer met.
cards.source: 8.8 - Introduction to loops and while statements

cards.text: Each execution of the loop's statements is called an {{c1::iteration}}.
cards.source: 8.8 - Introduction to loops and while statements

cards.text:
While loop syntax:
{{c1::while (condition)}}
{
    {{c2::// statements to repeat}}
}
cards.source: 8.8 - Introduction to loops and while statements

cards.front: When does a while loop check its condition?
cards.back: Before each iteration (including the first). If the condition is false initially, the loop body never executes.
cards.source: 8.8 - Introduction to loops and while statements

cards.question: What does this code print?
cards.explanation: The loop runs while count < 3. It prints 0, 1, 2, then when count becomes 3, the condition is false and the loop stops.
cards.source: 8.8 - Introduction to loops and while statements

cards.back: A loop whose condition never becomes false, causing it to run forever (or until the program is terminated).
cards.source: 8.8 - Introduction to loops and while statements

cards.text: An intentional infinite loop can be written as: {{c1::while (true)}} { }
cards.source: 8.8 - Introduction to loops and while statements

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Infinite loop - count is never incremented, so the condition is always true.
cards.explanation: The loop body doesn't modify count, so count < 5 is always true. This creates an infinite loop. You must update the loop variable inside the loop.
cards.source: 8.8 - Introduction to loops and while statements

cards.text: A variable that controls how many times a loop executes is called a {{c1::loop counter}} or {{c2::loop variable}}.
cards.source: 8.8 - Introduction to loops and while statements

cards.front: Should you declare the loop counter inside or outside the while loop?
cards.back: Outside, before the loop. While loops don't have a built-in initialization section (unlike for loops), so the counter must be declared beforehand.
cards.source: 8.8 - Introduction to loops and while statements

cards.explanation:
Every while loop needs three components to work correctly:
  • Initialization: Set up the loop counter (before the loop)
  • Condition: Test to determine if loop should continue
  • Update: Modify the loop counter (inside the loop)
Forgetting any component causes bugs - most commonly forgetting to update creates an infinite loop.
cards.source: 8.8 - Introduction to loops and while statements

cards.question: What does this code do?
cards.answer: Infinite loop - the semicolon after while creates a null statement.
cards.explanation: The semicolon after the condition makes the loop body empty (null statement). The block below is not part of the loop. This creates an infinite loop.
cards.source: 8.8 - Introduction to loops and while statements

cards.front: What happens if a while loop's condition is false on the first check?
cards.back: The loop body is skipped entirely and never executes. Execution continues with the statement after the loop.
cards.source: 8.8 - Introduction to loops and while statements
